attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with fever, rigors, and dysuria for two days. She has a medical history of recurrent urinary tract infections, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypertension. Her regular medications include metformin and perindopril. She completed a course of cefalexin prescribed by her general practitioner three days ago with no improvement. A urine sample taken three days ago showed >100 × 10^6/L leucocytes (0-10) and grew Escherichia coli resistant to ampicillin, cefalexin, ceftriaxone, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. Blood cultures have been taken and results are pending.

On examination, she appears severely unwell and confused. Her pulse rate is 125/min, blood pressure 85/50 mmHg, respiratory rate 28/min, SpO2 92% on room air, and temperature 39.2°C. She has suprapubic tenderness.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate antibiotic?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with severe sepsis, likely due to an ESBL-producing E. coli causing urosepsis. The resistance pattern showing resistance to multiple beta-lactam antibiotics (ampicillin and cephalosporins) is characteristic of ESBL production. Risk factors for ESBL infection include recent antibiotic use, recurrent urinary tract infections, and diabetes mellitus.

Meropenem is the most appropriate choice, as carbapenems remain the most reliable treatment for serious infections caused by ESBL-producing organisms. Carbapenems are stable against ESBL enzymes and provide excellent coverage against gram-negative organisms. The MERINO trial demonstrated that meropenem was superior to piperacillin-tazobactam for bloodstream infections caused by ceftriaxone-resistant E. coli or Klebsiella pneumoniae, with lower 30-day mortality.

Amoxicillin-clavulanate may retain activity against some ESBL-producing organisms. However, in this case of severe sepsis with shock, it would be an inappropriate choice.

Piperacillin-tazobactam is a broader-spectrum beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combination that may have activity against some ESBL-producing organisms. However, it is not recommended as first-line therapy for serious ESBL infections due to demonstrated inferior outcomes in clinical trials. The MERINO trial showed a 12.3% mortality rate with piperacillin-tazobactam compared to 3.7% with meropenem.

Ceftazidime, like other cephalosporins, would be ineffective against ESBL-producing organisms as these enzymes specifically hydrolyse the beta-lactam ring of cephalosporins.

Cefepime, although it is a fourth-generation cephalosporin with enhanced stability against some beta-lactamases, would still be ineffective against ESBL-producing organisms. ESBLs can hydrolyse the beta-lactam ring of all cephalosporins, including fourth-generation agents.

For uncomplicated urinary tract infections caused by ESBL-producing organisms, oral options like nitrofurantoin or fosfomycin may be appropriate. However, in this case of severe sepsis, immediate intravenous carbapenem therapy is required alongside appropriate resuscitation measures.
learningPoints: Extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL) producing organisms are resistant to most penicillins and cephalosporins.
Carbapenems are the first-line treatment for serious ESBL infections, with clinical trials showing superiority over piperacillin-tazobactam.
Risk factors for ESBL infection include recent antibiotic use, recurrent urinary tract infections, and diabetes mellitus.
references: Antibiotic. In: Therapeutic Guidelines. Melbourne: Therapeutic Guidelines Limited; accessed {26 Oct 2024}. https://www.tg.org.au

Harris PNA, Tambyah PA, Lye DC, et al. Effect of Piperacillin-Tazobactam vs Meropenem on 30-Day Mortality for Patients With E coli or Klebsiella pneumoniae Bloodstream Infection and Ceftriaxone Resistance: A Randomized Clinical Trial [published correction appears in JAMA. 2019 Jun 18;321(23):2370. doi: 10.1001/jama.2019.6706]. JAMA. 2018;320(10):984-994. doi:10.1001/jama.2018.12163 Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30208454/

Lee NY, Lee CC, Huang WH, Tsui KC, Hsueh PR, Ko WC. Carbapenem therapy for bacteremia due to extended-spectrum-β-lactamase-producing Escherichia coli or Klebsiella pneumoniae: implications of ertapenem susceptibility. Antimicrob Agents Chemother. 2012 Jun;56(6):2888-93. doi: 10.1128/AAC.06301-11. Epub 2012 Mar 19. PMID: 22430969; PMCID: PMC3370719. Available from: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3370719/

Gutiérrez-Gutiérrez B, Rodríguez-Baño J. Current options for the treatment of infections due to extended-spectrum beta-lactamase-producing Enterobacteriaceae in different groups of patients. Clin Microbiol Infect. 2019 Aug;25(8):932-942. doi: 10.1016/j.cmi.2019.03.030. Epub 2019 Apr 12. PMID: 30986558. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30986558/
